text: The transected proximal descending colon is visible, prepared for end colostomy formation during laparoscopic abdominoperineal resection for low rectal cancer. Below, the specimen is prepared for extraction.

Using a near-infrared enabled laparoscope, the surgeon examines the colostomy for perfusion sufficiency after systemic injection of indocyanine green (ICG). The colostomy appears dark, indicating poor fluorescence, while a small segment of mesocolon fluoresces brightly. The proximal colon shows better fluorescence, suggesting it would be a more suitable site for stoma formation.
The surgeon prepares to excise the poorly perfused segment of the colostomy before fashioning the stoma, aiming to prevent postoperative complications such as stenosis or necrosis.
Under white light, the surgeon examines the colostomy site, but the perfusion discrepancy visible in the near-infrared examination is not apparent.
